---
title: "Lecture: Triangulated Data Methods for Man-Made Disasters"
subtitle: "08:30 – 10:00 · May 19, 2026 · SPACERAISE @ GSSI"
author: "Elisabetta Pietrostefani, University of Liverpool"
date: "2026-05-19"
---

```{=html}
<div class="part-badge">Lecture · 90 min</div>
```

::: {.objectives}
**Learning objectives**

By the end of this lecture you will be able to:

- Define what we mean by *triangulated data methods* in the context of disaster research
- Contrast traditional and non-traditional (digital) data sources and their trade-offs
- Explain how satellite remote sensing is used to quantify physical damage
- Understand how survey and administrative data complement earth observation
- Situate the Beirut case study within a broader methodological literature
:::

## 1. What are man-made disasters?

*[Slides section 1 — typology, scale, examples]*

- Industrial accidents, explosions, conflict
- The Beirut port explosion as the running example

## 2. The data challenge

*[Slides section 2 — why standard data fails us]*

- Timeliness vs. depth
- Administrative collapse after disasters
- The role of remote sensing as a rapid-response tool

## 3. Traditional data sources

*[Slides section 3]*

- Household surveys: strengths, limitations, cost
- Administrative records: coverage gaps in crisis
- Census and registry data

## 4. Digital non-traditional data

*[Slides section 4]*

- Satellite imagery (optical, SAR)
- Social media and mobile data
- Nighttime lights as an economic proxy
- MAXAR and commercial very-high-resolution imagery

## 5. Triangulation logic

*[Slides section 5]*

- Why no single source is sufficient
- Convergent validity and divergent findings
- Spatial and temporal alignment challenges
- A framework for multi-source disaster analysis

## 6. The Beirut case study

*[Slides section 6 — bridge to the lab]*

- August 4, 2020 — what happened
- The damage footprint: spatial extent and intensity gradient
- Our data: MAXAR damage scores, in-person survey, synthetic outcomes
- Preview of the lab

## References

*References to be added.*
